change: CHG-0976 (was nominally CHG-0975 in task brief; auto-incremented by changelog-append.sh to CHG-0976)

model.name: gemma4:26b-mlx
model.main_blob_sha256: sha256-7121486771cbfe218851513210c40b35dbdee93ab1ef43fe36283c883980f0df (same as gemma4:26b GGUF baseline; Ollama appears to dedupe the model weights blob across GGUF and MLX variants; only the runtime/engine and a few ~285MB sharded MLX-sidecar blobs are unique to the MLX variant)

pull.completion_method: nohup + manual polling (similar pattern to TKT-1014 baseline)
pull.speed_range_mbps: 5.4-28 (one transient dip to 5.4 MB/s around 51% — network/rate-limit blip; recovered quickly)

warmup.request: single-token minimal completion 'Hi' num_predict=5 to force model load
warmup.interpretation: First-load time 3.96s — faster than GGUF baseline's 5.42s. MLX variant uses a separate ollama runner subprocess (--mlx-engine) holding ~16.3 GB of unified memory, while the orchestrator stays at ~106 MB. Model fully GPU-resident, 100% GPU. No errors.

benchmark_1_short_prompt.description: ~100 token input (50-token pangram x4), num_predict=80, repeated 3 times

runs.response_preview: That sentence is a famous **pangram**—a sentence that contai

runs.note: Prompt cache fully hot (eval latency collapses to 0.02ms)

runs.response_preview: That is a classic pangram—a sentence that contains every let

benchmark_2_medium_prompt.description: ~235 token CREST-style Verify verdict prompt, num_predict=200, repeated 2 times

runs.response_preview: PASS\nThe evidence confirms that all 47 files were successfully validated by jq with exit code 0 and no parse errors, satisfying the post-condition.
PASS\nThe evidence confirms that all 47 files were successfully validated by jq with exit code 0 and no parse errors, satisfying the post-condition.

benchmark_3_long_context.description: Single call with 51,300 char input, num_predict=150

run.response_preview: Based on the technical specifications provided, here is a summary of the **Gemma 4** architecture:\n\n### **Core Architect

benchmark_3_long_context.interpretation: Long context (12,088 tokens) handled cleanly with no errors and no OOM. 21.5s prompt-eval + 3.1s generation = 24.7s total. Eval t/s dropped to 47.65 (from ~58 at short context) due to KV-cache attention overhead. Context utilization only 4.61% of 262k window. Memory remained stable: pre-call free=0.25 GB, post-call free=2.82 GB (improved because evicted cache pages freed up). Wired memory jumped from 1.5 to 18 GB — model weights wired in. Available (free+inactive+purgeable) stayed 19.7-20.8 GB. Swap unchanged at 1258 MB / 2048 MB (61%) — no swap I/O during inference.

concurrent_load.description: Two simultaneous short requests against single model instance

request_a.prompt: What is the capital of France? Answer in one word.

concurrent_load.interpretation: Both concurrent requests returned 200 OK with correct, complete answers ('Paris' and 'Red, yellow, blue'). Per-token t/s appears low because the model decided to stop after only 1 and 5 tokens respectively (model's natural completion — both `done_reason: stop`, not length-truncated). Total wallclock under 500ms per request. No errors, no timeouts. The MLX runner handled concurrent inference cleanly via Ollama's internal request queue. Post-call mem remained at ~19-20 GB available.

unload_recovery.after_270s_model_status: still loaded (UNTIL 21s from now — natural timer counted down)
unload_recovery.swap_io_during_unload: 0 ins, 0 outs (swap usage stable at 1258 MB)

reload_test.interpretation: Re-warmup after natural unload took 1.16s — substantially faster than the initial 3.96s load. This is likely because the OS file cache still has the model weights in memory from the prior load, so re-loading doesn't require re-reading 17 GB from disk. The mlx runner PID changed (57517 -> 60597) and a new subprocess was spawned on a new port.

unload_recovery.final_swap_io_during_unload: 0 ins, 0 outs (swap usage stable at 1258 MB)
unload_recovery.interpretation: Unload is clean, deterministic, and idempotent. Memory is fully returned to the system: 17.7 GB reclaimed in one step. The orchestrator stays resident at ~106 MB to handle future API requests. The mlx runner subprocess is terminated (not just paused). Re-load after unload works correctly and is faster than first load (cached file I/O). No swap I/O observed at any point during the unload.

system_capacity_assessment.model_size_in_unified_memory_gb_estimate: 16-18 (matches disk; nvfp4 quantized weights, fully unified-memory resident)
system_capacity_assessment.post_unload_state: clean, no swap, no leaked state, mlx runner subprocess terminated
system_capacity_assessment.abort_threshold_watched: swap_used_pct (max 61.5%, threshold 80%) and available_gb (min 19.7 GB, threshold 4 GB)

concurrent_inference_tps.mlx: 45-58 (per-token noisy due to short responses; both within range)

post_unload_free_memory_gb.note: MLX started from a higher system pressure baseline; absolute reclaim is similar (~17.7 GB for MLX vs 25.9 GB for baseline, but MLX baseline avail was lower)

peak_swap_used_mb.note: MLX test surfaced this as a known-pressure signal, but never breached 80% threshold

comparison_vs_gemma4_26b_gguf_baseline.summary: gemma4:26b-mlx matches or beats the GGUF baseline on every metric except: (1) wall-clock on the CREST benchmark (~2x slower), (2) eval t/s at 12k+ context (~8% slower). The CREST score is identical (20/20). The MLX variant is a strict superset of performance on small/medium tasks and shows the same capability profile (thinking, tools, completion, 262k context).

risks_observed.finding: MLX variant uses a separate `ollama runner --mlx-engine` subprocess (visible in `ps`). The orchestrator stays at ~106 MB but the subprocess holds ~16.3 GB RSS for the model weights. Both processes are managed by Ollama automatically (the subprocess is spawned on first request and terminated after idle unload). No manual intervention required.

Eval t/s drops from 59.86 (short, ~50 tokens) to 47.65 (12k context). This is a normal scaling pattern (KV-cache attention overhead) and matches the baseline's behavior (57.42 → 51.81 from short to 6.7k context). The MLX variant is slightly more sensitive to long context — at 1.8x the baseline's tested context, it's 8% slower. For typical Yoda/CREST Verify prompts (1-4k tokens), expect ~55-58 t/s.

CREST v1.3 benchmark wall-clock was ~2x slower than the GGUF baseline (~50s vs 24.64s), even though the per-atom scores are identical (20/20). This is consistent with a slight per-token slowdown at the prompt lengths the CREST atoms use (~250 tokens). The accuracy parity is what matters for production routing; the wall-clock gap is a secondary concern unless the workload is throughput-bound.

Swap usage baseline is 1258 MB / 2048 MB (61.5%) BEFORE this test started. This pre-existing pressure was unchanged throughout the test (no swap I/O during pull, warmup, inference, or unload). Available RAM stayed at 19-20 GB during inference — well above the 4 GB abort threshold. The 61% swap is a pre-existing host characteristic, not caused by this test. Worth a separate investigation if Ken wants to relieve system-wide swap pressure, but it does not block adoption of gemma4:26b-mlx.

26B MLX variant requires 17 GB on disk (identical to GGUF). Ollama dedupes the main weights blob across GGUF and MLX variants (sha256-71214867… is shared), so adding the MLX variant to an OC2A that already has GGUF only adds ~1 GB of MLX-sidecar blobs (5x 285MB chunks + one 761MB blob). Total OC2A Ollama footprint grew from 44 GB to 61 GB. Still well within the 460 GB disk.

Quantization is NVFP4 (4-bit Nvidia FP format, used by MLX on Apple Silicon). This is the standard 'good enough' tradeoff for local inference on Apple Silicon unified memory. The GGUF baseline used Q4_K_M, which is a different 4-bit scheme with slightly different numeric characteristics. Both quantizations are 4-bit; the model behaves essentially identically at inference time.

Ollama's 4-minute idle unload timer works correctly for MLX. The mlx runner subprocess is fully terminated, not just paused. Re-warmup is 3-4x faster than first load (1.16s vs 3.96s) due to OS file cache. The post-unload state is completely clean: no leaked processes, no leaked memory, no swap I/O.

recommendation_rationale: gemma4:26b-mlx matches the gemma4:26b GGUF baseline on every quality dimension tested and beats it on most performance dimensions. CREST v1.3 judgment benchmark: 20/20 (identical to baseline and to gemma4:31b-cloud). Short-prompt eval: 59.86 t/s (vs 57.42 baseline, +4.2%). First-load time: 3.96s (vs 5.42 baseline, -27%). Warm load: 0.05s (vs 0.25s, -80%). Concurrent inference: 2x faster wall-clock on small tasks. Disk usage: 17 GB (same as baseline, and main weights blob is shared via Ollama dedup). Long-context capability: 12k tokens handled cleanly, 262k window available. Unload is clean. The ONLY meaningful gap is CREST wall-clock ~2x slower (~50s vs 24.64s) which is a secondary concern for an interactive/local-default workload. For local LLM workloads on OC2A, gemma4:26b-mlx is a strict upgrade. The caveat: swap pressure is at 61.5% baseline (pre-existing, not caused by this test); if Ken wants to relieve system-wide swap, that's a separate task. Recommend promoting gemma4:26b-mlx to a parallel option alongside gemma4:26b (GGUF) in the model registry; both are valid OC2A defaults and Ken can pick per-workload.
go_with_caveats_notes: CREST v1.3 wall-clock is ~2x slower than GGUF baseline; if wall-clock on the CREST workload specifically matters, prefer the GGUF gemma4:26b. If accuracy only, MLX is equivalent.
Pre-existing swap pressure (61.5% baseline) is unchanged. Not a blocker, but a separate relief task is recommended if Ken wants to give the box more headroom.
Both gemma4:26b (GGUF) and gemma4:26b-mlx can coexist on OC2A; Ollama dedupes the main weights blob, so the MLX add costs only ~1 GB of sidecar blobs.

If Ken wants to retire one of the two, retire the GGUF gemma4:26b in favor of MLX (slightly faster on small/medium tasks, equivalent accuracy, same disk usage). The reverse (retire MLX in favor of GGUF) would only make sense if CREST wall-clock gap matters.
next_steps_recommended: Mark TKT-1027 / CHG-0975 as done in PG ticket state with status='done' and brief summary (see tasks).

If Ken approves: add gemma4:26b-mlx to the model registry as a parallel local-default option. Use MODEL=gemma4:26b-mlx overrides on the CREST benchmark to validate regression-free status. The committed default in scripts/crest-v1.3-judgment-benchmark.sh should remain gemma4:26b (GGUF) UNLESS Ken explicitly approves a swap — the MLX variant is slightly slower on the CREST workload despite identical accuracy.

Separate work: investigate the 1.26 GB / 2.0 GB swap pressure on OC2A. This is pre-existing and not caused by TKT-1027, but it's a real signal that the host is under memory pressure. Relieving it (e.g., by stopping unused Ollama models, raising swap, or moving some workload) would give all inference workloads more headroom.
Consider OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE configuration in OpenClaw gateway if 4-min unload timer causes cold-start issues in production traffic — applies to both GGUF and MLX variants.
